videos.title: I built an AI agency system that closes clients on autopilot
videos.description: Breaking down the automation agency stack and the tools behind it.

transcript_segments.text: If you want to build an automation agency that actually gets clients, this workflow matters.
I am going to show the exact Claude and n8n stack we use to generate leads, book calls, and deliver support.
The body of the system starts with lead generation, CRM sync, and a multi agent routing layer.
We use MCP compatible tools, Airtable, Claude Code, and a simple RAG layer for client context.
The monetisation model is a monthly retainer plus setup fee, and I mention our accelerator course briefly.
What makes this work is positioning the offer as operational leverage instead of generic AI automation.
If you want the templates, I linked them below together with the course and a done for you implementation option.

videos.title: Claude Code for agency builders: the workflow nobody shows
videos.description: Technical breakdown of using Claude Code in service delivery.

transcript_segments.text: Most creators show flashy demos, but almost nobody shows how Claude Code fits into an actual client workflow.
This is the technical authority version with code review, deployment, and prompt management.
We walk through repositories, MCP servers, n8n triggers, and agent handoffs for customer support.
I also compare this stack with LangChain and explain when a simple Python script is enough.
The video closes with a brief mention of the agency playbook and a call to action for consulting.

videos.title: How to price AI automation for business clients in 2026
videos.description: Pricing frameworks, retainer models, and value-based selling for AI agencies.

transcript_segments.text: Pricing AI automation is one of the most common questions I get, so here is the full breakdown.
Most agency owners undercharge because they price on time instead of on outcomes.
I cover three models: project fee, monthly retainer, and productised service with a setup phase.
The consulting positioning section explains how to anchor value to revenue impact rather than hourly cost.
I close with a breakdown of our course pricing and how the community adds recurring revenue.

videos.title: Building a sales agent with GPT-4o and n8n
videos.description: Step-by-step tutorial for a GPT-4 powered outbound sales automation.

transcript_segments.text: Today I am building a full sales automation agent using GPT-4o and n8n, live on screen.
This agent handles cold email personalisation, follow-up scheduling, and CRM updates automatically.
The core workflow connects OpenAI, Airtable, and a Zapier webhook to trigger outreach sequences.
I walk through prompt engineering for tone, RAG for company research, and error handling in n8n.
The outro shows the dashboard reporting view and mentions a done-for-you agency service option.

videos.title: The MCP server setup every AI agency needs
videos.description: How to configure and host MCP servers for client-facing AI systems.

transcript_segments.text: MCP servers are becoming the standard interface for AI tools, and your agency needs to understand them.
I am going to show the full stack: MCP host, Claude, and a Python backend connected to Airtable.
The body covers server setup, tool registration, authentication, and connecting to existing CRM workflows.
I compare MCP with LangChain tool use and explain why MCP is the better long-term bet for multi-agent systems.
Closing section covers our accelerator cohort and a consulting offer for teams wanting a white-glove setup.

videos.title: Why your AI agency is not scaling (and how to fix it)
videos.description: Common scaling bottlenecks and operational fixes for AI service businesses.

transcript_segments.text: If your AI agency is stuck at the same revenue month after month, this is the video for you.
The three bottlenecks I see most are delivery, sales, and positioning — and they compound.
I walk through how to productise your service, automate delivery with n8n, and delegate with SOPs.
The retainer model section shows how to move clients from one-off projects to recurring revenue structures.
I close with the community and course offer and an invitation to book a consulting call.

videos.title: Cursor vs Claude Code for agency client work
videos.description: Head-to-head comparison for AI coding tools in a service delivery context.

transcript_segments.text: I have spent three months using both Cursor and Claude Code for client delivery work, so here is my honest take.
The short answer is that they are different tools for different workflow stages, not a direct swap.
Cursor wins on IDE integration and in-file editing speed, while Claude Code shines in multi-file orchestration and agentic tasks.
I show a real client project where I switched between them and explain the handoff points.
The video ends with my recommended stack and a mention of the agency playbook course.

videos.title: RAG pipelines for client knowledge bases: a practical guide
videos.description: Building retrieval-augmented generation systems for agency client use cases.

transcript_segments.text: RAG is not just a buzzword anymore. It is a real delivery component in most of the agency projects I run.
Today I build a complete RAG pipeline using Python, Supabase, and the Anthropic API.
I cover chunking strategies, embedding models, vector retrieval, and how to combine this with a Claude chat interface.
The observability section shows how to log retrieval results and evaluation scores so you can tune the system over time.
I end with a note on Pinecone versus Supabase vector and a discount code for the agency course.

videos.title: How I close high-ticket AI automation clients
videos.description: Sales call framework, objection handling, and closing strategies for AI agencies.

transcript_segments.text: Closing a five thousand dollar plus AI automation deal is different from closing a cheap website project.
In this video I walk through the exact discovery call framework we use to qualify and close high-ticket clients.
The framework covers pain identification, ROI framing, and building trust through a technical walkthrough.
I also cover cold email outreach sequences and how to use CRM automation to follow up without sounding like a bot.
I close with a note on the consulting offer and a link to the community for ongoing sales support.

videos.title: Multi-agent orchestration with LangGraph and Claude
videos.description: Building a LangGraph-based multi-agent system for complex automation tasks.

transcript_segments.text: Multi-agent orchestration is where AI automation goes from demo to real product, and LangGraph is my tool of choice.
Today I build a full multi-agent system with Claude, LangGraph, and a Python backend, all connected to Airtable.
I cover graph construction, agent routing, state management, and how to handle handoffs between specialised agents.
The observability section shows how to instrument agent calls with structured logging and evaluation hooks.
I end with performance benchmarks comparing this LangGraph setup to a simpler n8n workflow, then mention the cohort.

videos.title: n8n + MCP is the agent stack I would use if starting over
videos.description: Practical build for local-first AI workflows.

transcript_segments.text: If I had to restart my entire AI workflow stack, I would begin with n8n, MCP, and a boring database.
You do not need a huge framework to ship useful agent systems for customer support or lead gen.
The workflow starts with ingestion, routing, and small Python services rather than abstract orchestration.
Then we layer in evaluation, benchmark design, and observability using structured outputs.
I mention community templates and a paid cohort at the end, but the main point is architecture tradeoffs.
The closing section covers agent reliability, test harnesses, and what breaks in real deployments.

videos.title: Why most AI agent tutorials fail in production
videos.description: Critique of overfit demos and missing evaluation loops.

transcript_segments.text: Most AI agent tutorials fail because they optimise for a screenshot, not reliability.
This is anti corporate only in the sense that big claims hide bad engineering.
I break down evals, retries, prompt versioning, and why benchmark design matters.
There is also a section on agent memory, retrieval, and when a workflow should stay deterministic.
The outro points to a workshop and a code repository with templates.

videos.title: The evaluation framework I use for every AI agent I ship
videos.description: How to build systematic evals for production AI agents.

transcript_segments.text: Evaluation is the thing separating hobbyist AI projects from production systems, and most people skip it.
I am going to walk through the exact evaluation framework I apply before shipping any AI agent to a client.
The framework has three layers: unit tests for tools, integration tests for agent pipelines, and benchmark comparisons.
I show how to log agent traces, score outputs with an LLM judge, and track regression across deployments.
I end with a note on using GPT-4o as an automated evaluator and links to the community templates.

videos.title: Building reliable Python agents without LangChain
videos.description: Raw Python patterns for building durable AI agents without heavy frameworks.

transcript_segments.text: I stopped using LangChain for most projects about a year ago, and my code got significantly simpler.
Today I build a complete agent loop in raw Python with retries, structured outputs, and tool use via Anthropic.
The core pattern is a simple run loop with typed Pydantic models, error boundaries, and a state machine.
I add observability with structured logging, a SQLite trace store, and a minimal benchmark harness.
The outro includes links to the open-source template repo and an invite to the workshop.

videos.title: Observability for AI agents: what to log and why
videos.description: Practical guide to logging, tracing, and monitoring production AI agents.

transcript_segments.text: Observability for AI agents is an unsolved problem that most tutorials completely ignore.
By the end of this video you will have a logging schema and a trace viewer you can actually use.
I cover what to log at each agent step, how to structure traces in JSON, and how to correlate across runs.
The evaluation section shows how to feed trace data into an automated benchmark to catch regressions.
I close with a note on cost tracking per agent call and a link to the community Slack.

videos.title: Prompt versioning and testing in a team environment
videos.description: Git-based prompt management, regression testing, and team collaboration patterns.

transcript_segments.text: Prompt engineering without versioning is just guessing. Let me show you how to do it properly in a team.
The pattern I use is a simple YAML prompt registry with Git history and automated evaluation on every commit.
I cover the registry schema, a Python test harness that runs against Claude and GPT-4o, and CI integration.
The benchmark section shows how to detect prompt regressions before they reach production.
The outro links to the open-source repo and a workshop on advanced prompt engineering.

videos.title: Voice agents in 2026: architecture and pitfalls
videos.description: Building reliable voice AI agents with speech-to-text, LLM, and TTS pipelines.

transcript_segments.text: Voice agents are the next wave after text, and the architecture problems are completely different.
I am going to cover the full voice agent pipeline from STT to LLM to TTS with real latency numbers.
The core challenge is latency: STT adds 300ms, the LLM adds 800ms, and TTS adds another 400ms.
I show how to use streaming, parallel processing, and caching to get end-to-end latency under 1.5 seconds.
The closing section covers evaluation for voice agents and a community thread on real deployment failures.

videos.title: Designing agent memory that actually works
videos.description: Practical patterns for short-term, episodic, and semantic memory in AI agents.

transcript_segments.text: Agent memory is one of the most misunderstood components in the AI agent stack.
Today I break down three memory types: short-term context, episodic storage, and semantic retrieval, with code.
I build a working memory module in Python using a sliding context window, a SQLite episode store, and RAG.
The retrieval section shows how to combine Pinecone vector search with keyword filters for precision.
I end with benchmarks comparing memory configurations and a link to the open-source agent starter kit.

videos.title: When to use CrewAI versus raw Python agents
videos.description: Framework trade-offs for multi-agent systems in production.

transcript_segments.text: Everyone asks me whether to use CrewAI or just write their own agents. Here is my honest breakdown.
The answer depends on team size, iteration speed, and how much you care about observability and testing.
CrewAI gives you fast prototyping and built-in role definitions, but the abstractions get in the way at scale.
Raw Python agents are harder to start but easier to instrument, test, and maintain in a production environment.
I close with a decision tree and a note on the community discussion thread about framework choices.

videos.title: Fine-tuning small models for specialised agent tasks
videos.description: When and how to fine-tune open-source LLMs for reliable agent tool use.

transcript_segments.text: Fine-tuning is back on the table now that small open-source models are good enough for specialised agent tasks.
Today I walk through a complete fine-tuning run: data curation, training, evaluation, and production deployment.
I use a 7B model with QLoRA, curated tool-call examples, and a custom benchmark to measure improvement.
The evaluation section compares the fine-tuned model against GPT-4o on structured output tasks and tool routing.
I end with cost analysis showing when fine-tuning beats API calls and a link to the training dataset on GitHub.

videos.title: How I would sell AI lead generation in 2026
videos.description: Positioning, hooks, and outreach strategy for AI service offers.

transcript_segments.text: If I had to build an AI lead generation offer again, I would focus on positioning before tools.
This market is saturated with vague automation promises, so your hook has to target a real pain.
The body covers outreach, targeting, cold email systems, and CRM follow up with automation.
I compare agency, productised service, and course models with examples of where creators get stuck.
The close includes a consulting plug and a note on using AI demos carefully in sales calls.

videos.title: The white-space AI offer almost nobody is making
videos.description: Opportunity hunting in AI services.

transcript_segments.text: Everyone talks about generic AI automation, but almost nobody targets evaluation workflows for service businesses.
That gap matters because trust, QA, and reporting are where buyers actually feel pain.
I cover niche selection, reporting dashboards, and recurring revenue positioning.
There is a section on benchmark reports, reliability audits, and packaging the service offer.
The outro references a paid community and a lead magnet.

videos.title: Cold email for AI services: what is actually working in 2026
videos.description: Real data on cold email performance for AI automation agencies.

transcript_segments.text: Cold email open rates have dropped by 40 percent in two years, so the old playbook is broken.
I am going to share what is actually working for AI automation agencies based on data from our community.
The key changes are hyper-specific hooks, shorter copy, and proof-of-concept attachments instead of decks.
I walk through an outreach sequence using n8n and a GPT-4o personalisation layer for scale.
The outro includes templates you can use and a note on the consulting engagement for done-for-you outreach.

videos.title: AI content creation workflows for small teams
videos.description: How to use AI tools to build a scalable content production pipeline.

transcript_segments.text: A two-person team can now produce the content volume of a ten-person team with the right AI workflow.
I am going to show the exact content creation pipeline we use for our newsletter and YouTube channel.
The workflow uses Claude for research and drafts, Perplexity for fact-checking, and n8n for distribution.
I cover repurposing from long-form to short clips, social posts, and email sequences automatically.
I close with a note on the newsletter monetisation model and an offer for the content workflow course.

videos.title: How to position yourself as an AI specialist (not a generalist)
videos.description: Niche positioning strategy for AI consultants and agency owners.

transcript_segments.text: The biggest mistake I see AI consultants make is trying to serve every industry with every tool.
Positioning as a specialist triples your close rate and doubles what you can charge, and I have the data.
I walk through three positioning frameworks: vertical niche, use-case niche, and tool-stack specialisation.
The case study section shows a before and after of repositioning from generic to specialist and the revenue impact.
I close with a worksheet and an offer for a positioning sprint consulting call.

videos.title: Using Zapier and Make for client automation without code
videos.description: No-code automation tools for AI service delivery.

transcript_segments.text: Not every client needs a custom code solution. Zapier and Make handle 70 percent of real automation needs.
I am going to compare these two platforms and show when each is the right choice for an AI service offering.
I build three live automations: CRM lead routing, Slack notifications, and a reporting dashboard trigger.
The pricing section covers how to position these as retainer services rather than one-off project builds.
I end with a comparison table and a plug for the productised service workshop.

videos.title: The AI sales funnel that generated 50 leads in 30 days
videos.description: Step-by-step breakdown of an AI-powered inbound lead generation funnel.

transcript_segments.text: This funnel generated 50 qualified leads in 30 days for a B2B AI automation offer, and I am showing everything.
The system combines content creation, email nurture, and an AI chatbot qualification layer.
I walk through the lead magnet, the email sequence, and the chatbot built with n8n and Claude.
The analytics section shows open rates, click rates, and conversion at each stage of the funnel.
I close with a teardown of what I would change and an offer for a done-for-you funnel build.

videos.title: Building an AI customer support system for small businesses
videos.description: Practical guide to deploying AI customer support with escalation and handoffs.

transcript_segments.text: AI customer support is one of the most in-demand services I get asked to build for small business clients.
I am going to build a complete system with Claude, n8n, and a human escalation pathway, live on screen.
The build covers intent classification, RAG for knowledge base retrieval, and tone matching to brand guidelines.
I add a dashboard for monitoring unresolved tickets and a Slack alert when confidence drops below threshold.
The outro includes a retainer pricing template and an offer for a live build workshop.

videos.title: Why vibe coding is a business model, not just a trend
videos.description: How to productise AI-assisted code generation as a service.

transcript_segments.text: Vibe coding is getting dismissed as a toy by developers, but as a business model it is genuinely interesting.
I am going to make the case that code generation as a service is a real productised offer with strong margins.
The offer structure covers rapid MVP builds, iteration retainers, and a SaaS model for recurring revenue.
I show examples of clients paying for code generation workflows using Cursor, Claude Code, and GitHub Copilot.
I close with an analysis of the positioning risk and a note on the course for non-technical founders.

videos.title: Data extraction and scraping as an AI service offer
videos.description: How to package AI-powered data extraction as a recurring service for clients.

transcript_segments.text: Data extraction is one of the highest ROI services you can offer because every business needs structured data.
I am going to walk through how to package AI-powered scraping and extraction as a monthly retainer service.
The tech stack covers Python, Claude for extraction, and n8n for scheduling and delivery to client dashboards.
The business model section covers pricing based on data volume and the reporting dashboard add-on for upsells.
I close with legal considerations around scraping and an offer for the data services consulting sprint.
